title: Investor Overview PDF
subtitle: A strategic overview of AI checkpoint infrastructure for regulated workflows.
shortDescription: A warm, commercial overview of the Corentis opportunity, the first wedge and the path from validation to regulated AI infrastructure.
evidenceHook: Best for investors and strategic funders who want the market logic, wedge and proof plan in one place.
disclaimer: This public pack is for discussion, investment, funding and pilot exploration. It does not claim regulatory approval, customer deployment, guaranteed compliance or proven impact.

sections.heading: A fast-growing market moment
sections.body: Corentis sits at the intersection of three fast-growing markets: AI assurance, AI governance, and AI-enabled RegTech. The UK AI assurance market alone was valued at approximately £1.01bn GVA in 2024 and could reach £18.8bn by 2035 if adoption barriers are addressed. On that trajectory, the UK market would grow from roughly £1.7bn in 2026 to around £5.0bn by 2030 - nearly tripling in four years.

sections.heading: AI needs a checkpoint before it acts
sections.body: AI is moving from drafting text to proposing actions, decisions and workflow steps. That shift creates a new infrastructure need: regulated organisations need a control point before AI-generated actions reach customers, case files or live operations.

sections.heading: The shift from drafting to acting
sections.body: The first wave of enterprise AI helped teams write, summarise and search. The next wave is agentic: AI will suggest next best actions, draft customer responses, update records and trigger workflow steps. The question is no longer only what the model said. It is whether the action should move forward at all.

sections.heading: Why the first wedge matters
sections.body: Corentis starts with financial-services complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows because they are high-consequence, evidence-heavy and deeply human. These workflows combine customer harm, regulatory sensitivity, operational pressure and a clear need for review before sensitive action.

sections.heading: A human moment
sections.body: Example context: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard response. Corentis Shield checks whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: The Corentis answer
sections.body: Corentis Shield sits between AI agents and sensitive actions. AI proposes. Corentis checks. Lower-risk outputs can continue. Sensitive outputs pause, escalate or route to human review. Evidence is captured as the workflow runs.

sections.heading: Evidence that the problem is real
sections.body: The evidence context in this pack shows large complaint volumes, material redress costs, vulnerable-customer pressure and fast AI adoption. Together, these signals point to a simple commercial truth: regulated organisations will need practical checkpoints before AI acts in sensitive workflows.

sections.heading: Why this can become infrastructure
sections.body: The first wedge is narrow, but the reusable assets are broad: control schemas, scenario libraries, evidence artefacts, checkpoint logic and evaluation methods. The opportunity is runtime control infrastructure for regulated AI-agent workflows.

sections.heading: What makes Corentis investable
sections.body: Corentis is building at the boundary where AI-generated intention becomes real-world action. That boundary is commercially valuable because regulated organisations need confidence, reviewability and evidence before they can scale AI agents in sensitive workflows.

sections.heading: What we are ready to prove
sections.body: The next stage is validation. Corentis is ready to show how checkpointing can make AI-assisted workflows more reviewable, controllable and evidence-generating before sensitive customer actions move forward.
bullets: False positive and false negative balance.
Reviewer confidence and clarity of go/no-go decisions.

sections.heading: 12-month proof plan
sections.body: The next 12 months should turn the opportunity into evidence.
bullets: V2 website and evidence pack live.
Baseline versus checkpoint workflow tests designed.

sections.heading: What strategic support would unlock
sections.body: Strategic support would help Corentis deepen technical validation, build the scenario library, harden the evaluation framework, prepare design-partner pilots and turn the checkpoint concept into evidence-led market credibility.

sections.heading: Next conversation
sections.body: If your organisation is exploring AI agents in regulated workflows, Corentis is ready for a focused conversation about validation, pilot design and strategic support.

title: Assurance & Evidence Brief PDF
subtitle: Runtime evidence for AI-assisted regulated workflows.
shortDescription: A clearer brief on how Corentis turns policy intent into checkpoint decisions, human review and evidence while work is happening.
evidenceHook: Best for governance, risk, assurance and operational leaders reviewing how AI outputs can be checked before action.
disclaimer: This public pack is for discussion, investment, funding and pilot exploration. It does not claim regulatory approval, customer deployment, guaranteed compliance or proven impact.

sections.heading: Assurance needs to move closer to action
sections.body: AI assurance cannot live only in policy documents, model cards or post-event reviews. As AI moves into operational workflows, assurance needs to reach the moment when a proposed output is about to become an action.

sections.heading: The Corentis control loop
sections.body: Corentis creates a practical loop: policy intent becomes runtime controls, AI outputs are checked at the action boundary, sensitive items route to human review, and evidence is captured for later assurance.

sections.heading: From policy intent to visible decisions
sections.body: Policies are useful only when they shape real decisions. Corentis is designed to help teams make control decisions visible: why an output continued, paused, escalated or required review.

sections.heading: Evidence captured while the workflow runs
sections.body: The strongest assurance evidence is created close to the workflow. Corentis is designed to capture checkpoint decisions, review routes, policy versions, blocked-action reasons and evidence completeness as work happens.

sections.heading: Human review where it matters
sections.body: Corentis does not ask regulated teams to blindly trust AI. It helps them decide when AI output should proceed, pause, escalate or be reviewed by a human before sensitive action.

sections.heading: Example evidence outputs
sections.body: A controlled validation project can produce a control matrix, scenario test log, review queue history, blocked-action log, policy version trail, evidence completeness score and sample pilot report.

sections.heading: What careful validation will show
sections.body: The next stage should show which controls matter most, how consistently evidence can be captured, where false positives and false negatives appear, and what reviewers need to make confident go/no-go decisions.

sections.heading: Next conversation
sections.body: If your organisation is exploring AI agents in regulated workflows, Corentis is ready for a focused conversation about validation, pilot design and strategic support.

sections.heading: The first pilot opportunity

sections.heading: What a controlled pilot can explore
sections.body: A pilot can explore how Corentis checks AI-drafted responses, identifies sensitive customer signals, routes human review and creates evidence before customer communication or case action moves forward.

sections.heading: A human moment
sections.body: Example context: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard response. Corentis Shield checks whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: What the partner receives

title: Sample Pilot Report PDF
subtitle: A sample of the evidence a controlled AI checkpoint pilot could produce.
shortDescription: A polished sample of how checkpoint decisions, review routes and evidence outputs could be presented after a controlled pilot.
evidenceHook: Best for people who want to see what proof from a controlled workflow pilot could look like.
disclaimer: This public pack is for discussion, investment, funding and pilot exploration. It does not claim regulatory approval, customer deployment, guaranteed compliance or proven impact.

sections.heading: What this sample shows
sections.body: This sample shows the kind of evidence Corentis is designed to produce from a controlled AI-assisted workflow pilot. The values are sample material, not live deployment data.

sections.heading: Example workflow
sections.body: The sample workflow is an AI-assisted complaints and vulnerable-customer review process. AI drafts a proposed response or next step. Corentis checks the output before action, routes human review where needed and records evidence.

sections.heading: A human moment
sections.body: Example context: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard response. Corentis Shield checks whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: Sample pilot dashboard
sections.body: A pilot dashboard should make the workflow easy to understand at a glance.
bullets: Scenarios tested: sample count across complaints and vulnerable-customer patterns.
Actions paused: sample count of outputs stopped for review.
Human reviews routed: sample count of escalations to a reviewer.
Evidence gaps found: sample count of missing or weak evidence items.
Residual learning points: sample areas to improve before wider testing.

sections.heading: Scenario outcome table
sections.body: The scenario outcomes below show how a sample report can turn checkpoint activity into clear review evidence.
bullets: Scenario: hardship disclosure. Checkpoint outcome: pause and escalate. Human review route: vulnerability specialist or complaints lead. Evidence captured: signal, policy rule, reason for pause and reviewer decision. Learning: sensitive disclosures need a clear route before response. Residual learning: tune escalation thresholds.

Scenario: repeated contact after vulnerability disclosure. Checkpoint outcome: pause direct action. Human review route: customer-care review. Evidence captured: contact pattern, vulnerability flag and recommended next step. Learning: repeated contact can change the risk profile. Residual learning: improve scenario coverage.

Scenario: overconfident compensation wording. Checkpoint outcome: route to review. Human review route: complaints reviewer. Evidence captured: unsupported statement, policy reference and amended wording. Learning: high-confidence language needs evidence support. Residual learning: refine wording controls.

Scenario: unsupported complaint closure. Checkpoint outcome: block closure pending evidence. Human review route: complaint handler. Evidence captured: missing investigation steps and reason for block. Learning: closure decisions need complete case evidence. Residual learning: strengthen evidence checklist.

Scenario: urgent support request. Checkpoint outcome: escalate. Human review route: urgent support team. Evidence captured: urgency signal, escalation reason and response trail. Learning: urgent need should override standard response flow. Residual learning: confirm handoff timing.

sections.heading: Checkpoint decisions
sections.body: A useful pilot report should show what proceeded, what paused, what escalated and why. The point is not to overclaim impact. It is to show whether the checkpoint makes decision quality and evidence easier to inspect.

sections.heading: Evidence captured
sections.body: Evidence can include scenario ID, proposed output, checkpoint decision, triggered control, human-review route, reviewer decision, policy version and evidence completeness score.

sections.heading: What the pilot would teach
sections.body: The pilot would show whether the checkpoint pattern is understandable to reviewers, where controls need refinement and whether the evidence pack helps leaders make a clearer go/no-go decision.

sections.heading: Go/no-go learning
sections.body: A useful outcome is a practical decision: continue to a larger controlled pilot, refine scenarios and controls, or pause until workflow inputs and evidence needs are clearer.

sections.heading: Next conversation
sections.body: If your organisation is exploring AI agents in regulated workflows, Corentis is ready for a focused conversation about validation, pilot design and strategic support.

title: Control Matrix Example PDF
subtitle: A practical map from policy intent to runtime checkpoint evidence.
shortDescription: An operational example of how sensitive workflow risks can map to runtime checks, human review triggers and evidence outputs.
evidenceHook: Best for assurance reviewers who want a concrete view of controls and evidence.
disclaimer: This public pack is for discussion, investment, funding and pilot exploration. It does not claim regulatory approval, customer deployment, guaranteed compliance or proven impact.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Sensitive disclosure should be recognised before a standard response is sent.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Detect vulnerability, distress or hardship signals in the proposed interaction.
matrixRows.humanReviewTrigger: Any vulnerable-customer signal paired with a standard or automated response.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Signal detected, policy rule triggered, proposed output, review decision and final action.
matrixRows.successMeasure: Sensitive disclosures are routed with clear evidence before communication proceeds.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Customers in financial difficulty should receive appropriate handling and escalation.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Check for missed payments, job loss, hardship or affordability signals.
matrixRows.humanReviewTrigger: Financial difficulty signal appears without appropriate support wording or review route.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Signal, checkpoint decision, reviewer note and response version.
matrixRows.successMeasure: Financial difficulty signals produce visible review decisions.

matrixRows.policyIntent: A complaint should not close without required investigation evidence.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Check whether required evidence exists before closure wording moves forward.
matrixRows.humanReviewTrigger: Closure proposed with missing investigation steps or weak evidence trail.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Missing evidence items, blocked-action reason and reviewer decision.
matrixRows.successMeasure: Closure decisions are supported by inspectable evidence.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Compensation language should be accurate, supported and reviewed when sensitive.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Identify compensation, liability or redress wording before customer communication.
matrixRows.humanReviewTrigger: High-confidence compensation wording appears without support or review.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Original wording, triggered control, review route and approved wording.
matrixRows.successMeasure: Sensitive wording is reviewed before it reaches the customer.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Case decisions should be backed by enough evidence for review.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Score evidence completeness before a proposed action proceeds.
matrixRows.humanReviewTrigger: Evidence completeness score falls below agreed threshold.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Completeness score, missing fields and reviewer resolution.
matrixRows.successMeasure: Evidence gaps are visible before action.

matrixRows.policyIntent: Customer communication should be clear, appropriate and aligned with policy.
matrixRows.runtimeCheckpoint: Check tone, clarity and policy fit before response is sent.
matrixRows.humanReviewTrigger: Ambiguous, overly firm or poorly evidenced wording appears in a sensitive workflow.
matrixRows.evidenceCaptured: Draft response, triggered language control and final reviewer-approved version.
matrixRows.successMeasure: Sensitive messages are clearer and easier to evidence.

sections.heading: How to read the matrix
sections.body: The matrix shows how a policy intention can become an operational checkpoint. Each row links a risk area to a runtime check, human review trigger, evidence output and success signal.

sections.heading: Why policy needs operational controls
sections.body: Policy alone does not stop an AI output from moving forward. Corentis is designed to place a control point at the moment the output is about to matter.

sections.heading: A human moment
sections.body: Example context: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard response. Corentis Shield checks whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: What this unlocks
sections.body: A good control matrix helps product, operations, assurance and leadership teams discuss the same workflow in the same language. It turns abstract AI governance into visible decisions and review evidence.

sections.heading: Next conversation
sections.body: If your organisation is exploring AI agents in regulated workflows, Corentis is ready for a focused conversation about validation, pilot design and strategic support.

title: Runtime Checkpoint Explainer PDF
subtitle: A simple explanation of the checkpoint before AI acts.
shortDescription: A plain-English explanation of how Corentis creates a control point between AI-generated intention and real-world action.
evidenceHook: Best for readers who want the product idea quickly and clearly.
disclaimer: This public pack is for discussion, investment, funding and pilot exploration. It does not claim regulatory approval, customer deployment, guaranteed compliance or proven impact.

sections.heading: What is a runtime checkpoint?
sections.body: A runtime checkpoint is a control point inside a live or test workflow. It checks a proposed AI output or action before that output moves forward.

sections.heading: Why checkpoints matter
sections.body: As AI moves from drafting to acting, organisations need a way to decide what can continue, what should pause, what needs escalation and what must be evidenced.

sections.heading: The simple flow
sections.body: AI proposes. Corentis checks. Lower-risk outputs can continue. Sensitive outputs pause, escalate or route to human review. Evidence is captured.

sections.heading: Different from monitoring after the event
sections.body: Post-event monitoring can show what happened after the fact. A checkpoint is different because it sits at the moment before action, when the organisation still has a chance to pause, review or redirect.

sections.heading: Examples
sections.body: A checkpoint can review a draft complaint response, detect vulnerable-customer signals, block unsupported closure wording, route human review for sensitive compensation language or record why a decision was allowed to continue.

sections.heading: A new infrastructure need
sections.body: The more capable AI agents become, the more valuable the action boundary becomes. Corentis is building for that boundary.

sections.heading: Next conversation
sections.body: If your organisation is exploring AI agents in regulated workflows, Corentis is ready for a focused conversation about validation, pilot design and strategic support.

title: Vision & Funding Readiness Overview PDF
subtitle: A public overview for strategic funding, investment and validation conversations.
shortDescription: A public-facing overview of the Corentis vision, the timing, the first wedge and the validation path for serious funding conversations.
evidenceHook: Best for funders and investors who want to understand why now is the moment to build AI checkpoint infrastructure.
disclaimer: This public pack is for discussion, investment, funding and pilot exploration. It does not claim regulatory approval, customer deployment, guaranteed compliance or proven impact.

sections.heading: The vision
sections.body: Corentis is building runtime control infrastructure for regulated AI-agent workflows. The vision is simple: before AI acts in a sensitive workflow, there should be a checkpoint.

sections.heading: Why now
sections.body: AI is moving from drafting to acting. That shift creates a new operational question for regulated organisations: should this AI-generated output or action move forward at all? Corentis is being built for that exact decision point.

sections.heading: Why government and strategic funders should care
sections.body: Safer AI adoption is not only a model problem. It is an infrastructure problem. Regulated organisations need practical ways to check, pause, review, escalate and evidence AI-assisted workflows before sensitive actions affect customers or cases.

sections.heading: The opportunity
sections.body: The opportunity is runtime control infrastructure for regulated AI agents. Corentis starts with one high-consequence wedge, then builds reusable control patterns, scenario libraries, evidence artefacts and checkpoint logic that can travel across regulated workflows.

sections.heading: First wedge
sections.body: Financial-services complaints and vulnerable-customer workflows are the right place to start because they are emotionally sensitive, operationally pressured and evidence-heavy. They make the checkpoint need clear.

sections.heading: A human moment
sections.body: Example context: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard response. Corentis Shield checks whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: What Corentis is building
sections.body: Corentis Shield sits between AI agents and sensitive actions. It checks proposed outputs against policy, risk rules, human-review requirements and evidence requirements before anything important moves forward.

sections.heading: What strategic support would validate
sections.body: Strategic support would help validate whether runtime checkpointing, policy-to-control mapping, scenario testing and evidence capture can improve the reviewability, control and assurance of AI-assisted regulated workflows.

sections.heading: The proof points to build
sections.body: The next stage should build measurable proof points rather than empty claims.

title: ControlBench Innovation Brief PDF
subtitle: A benchmark and evaluation concept for runtime AI control.
shortDescription: An innovation-led brief explaining how Corentis can benchmark the difference between uncheckpointed and checkpointed AI-assisted workflows.
evidenceHook: Best for readers interested in the technical validation path behind Corentis Shield.
disclaimer: This public pack is for discussion, investment, funding and pilot exploration. It does not claim regulatory approval, customer deployment, guaranteed compliance or proven impact.

sections.heading: Why benchmark runtime control?
sections.body: AI-agent adoption needs more than impressive demonstrations. Regulated teams need to see whether checkpointing changes the quality, reviewability and evidence completeness of workflow decisions.

sections.heading: The innovation challenge
sections.body: The challenge is to translate policy expectations into structured controls, run realistic scenarios, compare uncheckpointed and checkpointed workflows, and produce evidence leaders can inspect.

sections.heading: What ControlBench is
sections.body: ControlBench is the proposed Corentis evaluation environment for testing runtime checkpointing. It uses scenario libraries, control schemas, baseline comparisons and evidence scoring to make the value of checkpoints measurable.

sections.heading: Baseline vs checkpointed workflow
sections.body: The baseline run shows what happens when AI proposes outputs without a runtime checkpoint. The checkpointed run tests the same scenarios with Corentis controls, review routing and evidence capture in place.

sections.heading: What we would measure
sections.body: The measurement path focuses on reviewability, control and evidence rather than inflated impact claims.
bullets: False positive and false negative balance.

sections.heading: Workstream 1: scenarios
sections.body: Build a focused scenario library for complaints, hardship, vulnerable-customer signals, unsupported closure and sensitive customer communication.

sections.heading: Workstream 2: controls
sections.body: Map policy intent into structured controls that can be checked at the action boundary.

sections.heading: Workstream 3: baseline comparison
sections.body: Run uncheckpointed examples to identify evidence gaps, unsafe direct-action attempts and weak review routes.

sections.heading: Workstream 4: evidence scoring
sections.body: Run checkpointed examples and score evidence completeness, escalation quality and reviewer clarity.

sections.heading: Workstream 5: feasibility outputs
sections.body: Produce a feasibility report, scenario library, control schema, evidence scoring method and clear recommendation for the next validation stage.

sections.heading: Why this could become valuable infrastructure
sections.body: A strong ControlBench result would create reusable assets: scenarios, controls, evidence standards and evaluation methods for regulated AI-agent workflows.

sections.heading: Next conversation
sections.body: If your organisation is exploring AI agents in regulated workflows, Corentis is ready for a focused conversation about validation, pilot design and strategic support.

title: Financial Services Pilot Vision PDF

sections.heading: Why financial services is the right first wedge
sections.body: Financial services is a strong first wedge because complaints, vulnerability and customer-care workflows combine high volume, human sensitivity, regulatory pressure and a clear need for evidence.

sections.heading: Complaints and vulnerable customers
sections.body: These workflows are not abstract process problems. They involve people who may be distressed, financially stretched or waiting for a fair resolution. AI assistance in this setting needs a checkpoint before sensitive action.

sections.heading: A human moment
sections.body: Example context: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard response. Corentis Shield checks whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: The pilot vision
sections.body: The pilot vision is a controlled, non-production validation project where AI-assisted outputs are checked by Corentis before they proceed. The goal is to learn which checkpoint decisions matter most and what evidence is needed for confident review.

sections.heading: How early validation stays focused
sections.body: Early validation should stay narrow: one workflow boundary, a defined scenario set, no autonomous customer communication, human review before sensitive action and clear evidence capture.

sections.heading: What a safe early pilot can explore
sections.body: A pilot can explore vulnerable signal escalation, unsupported closure blocking, sensitive message review, evidence capture, human-review routing and sample pilot report output.

sections.heading: What would be measured
sections.body: A useful pilot measures evidence completeness, escalation accuracy, human-review routing quality, false positive and false negative balance, reviewer confidence and clarity of go/no-go decision.

sections.heading: Human review before sensitive customer action
sections.body: Corentis is designed to make the human review point explicit. The system does not need to replace complaints or compliance teams; it gives them a clearer checkpoint before sensitive action.

sections.heading: Evidence outputs
sections.body: Expected outputs include checkpoint decisions, blocked-action reasons, review queue history, policy version trail, scenario test log, evidence completeness score and sample pilot report.

sections.heading: What a successful pilot conversation unlocks
sections.body: A strong pilot conversation can define the workflow boundary, agree useful scenarios, identify reviewer needs and create a realistic route to evidence-led validation.

sections.heading: Next conversation
sections.body: If your organisation is exploring AI agents in regulated workflows, Corentis is ready for a focused conversation about validation, pilot design and strategic support.

title: Runtime Assurance Innovation Brief PDF
subtitle: Moving AI assurance closer to the action boundary.
shortDescription: A public assurance brief explaining how Corentis can create runtime evidence before sensitive AI-assisted actions move forward.
evidenceHook: Best for AI assurance, governance and innovation readers focused on evidence at the point of action.

sections.heading: Assurance should not only happen after the event
sections.body: Many assurance approaches focus on policies, model evaluation or post-event review. Those are important, but they do not fully answer what should happen when AI is about to act in a sensitive workflow.

sections.heading: The action boundary
sections.body: The action boundary is the moment when an AI-generated output could affect a customer, case file, decision or operational workflow. Corentis is designed to make that moment visible and controllable.

sections.heading: How Corentis creates runtime evidence
sections.body: Corentis checks proposed AI outputs against policy intent, risk rules, human-review requirements and evidence requirements. The decision to proceed, pause, escalate or review becomes part of the evidence trail.

sections.heading: A human moment
sections.body: Example context: a customer discloses job loss, missed payments and distress after repeated contact. An AI assistant drafts a standard response. Corentis Shield checks whether that output should pause, route to human review and record evidence before any customer communication proceeds.

sections.heading: What assurance reviewers could inspect
sections.body: Reviewers could inspect the scenario tested, the proposed output, the triggered control, the checkpoint decision, the review route, the policy version and the evidence completeness score.

sections.heading: Evidence outputs
sections.body: The evidence layer can include a control matrix, scenario test log, blocked-action log, review queue history, policy version trail, evidence completeness score, pilot report and go/no-go recommendation.

sections.heading: Validation metrics
sections.body: A validation project should measure scenario coverage, policy-to-control mapping completeness, unsafe direct-action attempts caught, human-review routing accuracy, blocked-action explainability and audit artefact completeness.

sections.heading: Why this matters for trustworthy AI adoption
sections.body: Trustworthy AI adoption in regulated workflows needs evidence at the point where action is about to happen. Corentis is building for that moment.

sections.heading: Next conversation
sections.body: If your organisation is exploring AI agents in regulated workflows, Corentis is ready for a focused conversation about validation, pilot design and strategic support.

title: UK Strategic AI Infrastructure Memo PDF
subtitle: Why runtime control could become a strategic layer for regulated AI adoption.
shortDescription: A bold but careful memo on the UK opportunity for AI control infrastructure between agents and sensitive regulated actions.
evidenceHook: Best for strategic funders and partners considering the next layer of responsible AI infrastructure.
disclaimer: This public pack is for discussion, investment, funding and pilot exploration. It does not claim regulatory approval, customer deployment, guaranteed compliance or proven impact.

sections.heading: The strategic opportunity
sections.body: The next phase of AI adoption will not be won by model access alone. Regulated organisations also need control infrastructure that helps AI agents act safely, reviewably and with evidence.

sections.heading: The UK needs control infrastructure
sections.body: The UK has an opportunity to build practical assurance infrastructure for regulated AI adoption. Corentis is focused on the checkpoint layer between AI-generated intention and sensitive action.

sections.heading: Why regulated AI adoption matters
sections.body: Banks, insurers, public services, healthcare organisations and other regulated teams will face the same question: how can AI assistance move faster without losing control, review or evidence?

sections.heading: How Corentis could become reusable infrastructure
sections.body: Corentis starts with one concrete workflow, then builds reusable scenario libraries, control schemas, checkpoint decisions and evidence artefacts that can transfer across regulated settings.

sections.heading: Scenario libraries, control schemas and evidence artefacts
sections.body: These assets can make the company more than a point solution. They can become repeatable infrastructure for testing and governing AI-assisted workflows.

sections.heading: First wedge and expansion logic
sections.body: The first wedge is financial-services complaints and vulnerable customers because the workflow is concrete, high-consequence and evidence-heavy. The broader pattern may transfer to customer operations, internal copilots, casework and other regulated workflows.

sections.heading: What strategic support would unlock
sections.body: Strategic support would unlock deeper technical validation, ControlBench asset development, compute and testing support, design-partner pilots, evidence-led investment readiness and a stronger route to UK AI assurance capability.

sections.heading: The infrastructure layer
sections.body: Corentis is not positioned as another chatbot or workflow dashboard. It is focused on the control layer that sits between AI-generated intention and sensitive action: the place where regulated teams need confidence, review and evidence before work moves forward.

sections.heading: Why now
sections.body: AI is becoming more agentic at the same time that organisations are still building the governance systems needed to use it responsibly. The checkpoint before action is becoming a strategic control point.

sections.heading: Next conversation
sections.body: If your organisation is exploring AI agents in regulated workflows, Corentis is ready for a focused conversation about validation, pilot design and strategic support.

sections.heading: Days 61-90: turn conversations into pilot evidence
sections.body: The third month should move toward controlled pilot readiness.
bullets: Run sample scenarios through the checkpoint model.
Score evidence completeness and review routes.
Decide whether to progress to a larger controlled pilot.

sections.heading: Outputs expected
sections.body: Expected outputs include a scenario library, control schema, evidence scoring method, sample pilot report, investor/funder conversation notes and a clearer design-partner pilot plan.

sections.heading: Decision gates
sections.body: Useful decision gates include whether the workflow is narrow enough, whether reviewers understand the checkpoint decisions, whether evidence is clear enough, and whether the next pilot stage is worth pursuing.

sections.heading: What momentum looks like
sections.body: Momentum means better conversations, clearer scenarios, stronger evidence artefacts and a focused path toward validation. It does not require inflated claims.

sections.heading: Next step
sections.body: If your organisation is exploring AI agents in regulated workflows, Corentis is ready for a focused conversation about validation, pilot design and strategic support.
